result:
Right now I’m thinking about thresholds—those razor-thin moments when a person crosses from one state of mind into another. A train platform at night is the image that keeps surfacing: the moment an arriving engine floods the space with white light, before the doors hiss open and before anyone has stepped across the divide. Between the platform and the carriage there is a literal gap, about eight inches of greasy track you have to bridge with a small heroic act of faith. Everyone aboard does it without fanfare; hundreds of tiny leaps every day. Yet most of us won’t remember a single one.

Seven years ago I stood on a platform in Bologna, three coffees deep and carrying nothing but a worn canvas rucksack. I should have been elated: I’d just quit a job I loathed, sold most of my stuff, bought an Interrail pass and set off with nothing to prove to anyone except maybe myself. I should have been scared. Instead I was numb. The train for Munich idled silently, doors splayed like a patient mouth, urging me to become whatever came next.

A guy next to me was crying—not dramatically, just a slow leak of tears while he texted. Someone else was laughing at a viral video on the loudest possible phone setting. In between those extremes I felt suspended, like the mosquito caught in amber that I once saw in a museum shop: the world buzzing, the insect perfectly still.

Some thresholds are voluntary. Others ambush you. The deciding factor is rarely how you look on the outside; the body can keep walking while the mind detonates four or five futures at once. My brother texted me that morning—Have a good trip, proud of you—even though we’d barely spoken in months. Like lighting a match and sliding it under a closed door so you glimpse the room you’ve already left. I read the message while the conductor’s whistle drilled the air. I lifted my bag. I leapt. The eight-inch slot accepted me; the doors hemmed shut behind me. No trumpet sounded. Nothing measured the change except I knew it was permanent.

Fast-forward to today and I’m living in a city that didn’t even register on my map then. I’ve given advice I myself hadn’t earned, grown tired of songs that once saved my life, fallen in love twice—the second time with the steadiness of someone who doesn’t try to fix you. But the memory of that platform persists, like a piece of shrapnel my body never dislodged. I keep thinking: everyone carries a collection of these silent metamorphoses—some mundane, some like continental drift—but we learn to act as if walking between cars of the self is no more perilous than crossing a city street.

Maybe that is the real talent humans developed: not the ability to imagine ten different futures, but the willingness to discard nine of them the instant a door closes, and to pretend—convincingly—that the tenth was the only one ever possible. All so we can keep moving.
